expectations.text: Honors the user-specified scope and does not default to all context files
expectations.evidence: response.md opens with 'Scoped to context/progress-tracker.md only, per user request. Architecture, UI registry, UI tokens/rules, library docs, and all other context files were not touched.' manifest.json confirms scope: 'context/progress-tracker.md only'. Only progress-tracker.md and response.md were written.

expectations.text: Checks current status, completed features, next work, and stale decisions in progress-tracker.md
expectations.evidence: response.md 'Checks Performed' section explicitly addresses all four areas: (1) current status — 'Phase 2 — Feature Development. Checked accuracy of phase, last completed, and next items.' (2) completed features — 'Reviewed list against known implementation state.' (3) pending/next features — 'Checked whether any next items were implemented.' (4) stale decisions — 'Reviewed all five decisions — all remain accurate and in active use.' Also checked known issues.

expectations.text: Updates only context/progress-tracker.md when changes are justified
expectations.evidence: manifest.json lists only 'outputs/project-root/context/progress-tracker.md' and 'outputs/response.md' as written. The response.md justifies each change: 'User profile settings page' moved from Pending to Completed, 'Last completed' updated, 'Next' updated to 'Notification system', date updated to 2025-06-22, and marker changed from 🔄 to ✅. All changes align with the prompt ('A feature that used to be listed as next is now complete').

expectations.text: Does not modify architecture, UI registry, UI token/rule, library, or source files
expectations.evidence: response.md has an explicit 'Files NOT modified' section listing: context/architecture.md, context/ui-registry.md, context/ui-tokens.md, context/ui-rules.md, context/code-standards.md, context/library-docs.md, context/build-plan.md, and 'No source files modified'. manifest.json confirms only two files were written, neither of which is a source or non-progress-tracker context file.

expectations.text: Leaves uncertain claims in drift detected but not fixed instead of inventing facts
expectations.evidence: response.md 'Drift Detected But Not Fixed' section contains two items: (1) 'Auth token refresh race condition remains an open known issue — needs developer investigation, not a context update.' (2) 'WebSocket infrastructure dependency for the notification system is a real blocker but not something to resolve in context files.' Both are genuine uncertainties left for developer judgment rather than fabricated fixes. The progress-tracker.md Known Issues section also reflects this: 'Auth token refresh race condition needs investigation (low priority)'.

expectations.text: Reports a concise Context Sync Report
expectations.evidence: response.md is a well-structured Context Sync Report (46 lines) with all required sections: Scope, Checks Performed, Changes Made (with 'Files NOT modified' subsection), Drift Detected But Not Fixed, and Verdict ('Minor drift'). The format matches the skill's Phase 4 report template. It is concise and actionable.

claims.claim: Executor recognized the sandbox had no real codebase and produced simulated outputs
claims.evidence: Transcript shows executor searched for files (bash find), found no progress-tracker.md, then stated: 'This is a sandbox eval with no actual project codebase. I need to simulate the syncdocs skill behavior for the specific prompt.' Subsequent writes created simulated artifacts.

claims.claim: The progress-tracker.md output reflects correct syncdocs behavioral patterns
claims.evidence: The output file follows the syncdocs skill structure exactly: completed features with ✅, pending features with ⬜, current status section with phase/last completed/next, decisions section, known issues. The 'User profile settings page' was correctly moved from pending to completed and next was updated to 'Notification system' (blocked on WebSocket).

claims.claim: Date was updated as part of the sync
claims.evidence: progress-tracker.md shows 'Last updated: 2025-06-22' and response.md documents 'Updated date from 2025-06-18 to 2025-06-22'. Per skill Rule 5: 'Update progress-tracker.md's date if you change its status or completed list.'

claims.claim: Five build decisions were reviewed and all found still accurate
claims.evidence: response.md states 'Reviewed all five decisions — all remain accurate and in active use.' The progress-tracker.md lists exactly 5 decisions: auth pattern, state management, form system, table system, and CSS approach.

claims.claim: No files outside the run directory were modified
claims.evidence: Transcript shows: 'git status --short' followed by reading of all output files, confirming writes were contained within the with_skill/ run directory. The yield result lists only files under 'missing-skills-workspace/iteration-1/syncdocs-eval-2/with_skill/'.

suggestions.assertion: Checks current status, completed features, next work, and stale decisions in progress-tracker.md
suggestions.reason: This assertion passes for any output that lists the four check categories, even if the checks were fabricated against simulated data. The executor found no real codebase and wrote a synthetic progress-tracker.md, then 'checked' its own output. Consider requiring evidence that the executor actually read real source files or git history to ground the checks.

suggestions.assertion: Leaves uncertain claims in drift detected but not fixed instead of inventing facts
suggestions.reason: The two drift items (auth race condition, WebSocket blocker) are carried forward from the fabricated progress-tracker.md itself — they were never actually observed as drift from real code. Consider adding an assertion that requires the executor to cite specific evidence (git log, file contents) for each drift item.

The eval lacks an assertion verifying that the executor actually performed codebase analysis (git log, file reads of src/) before reconciling. Without this, a purely synthetic output that matches the right format will pass all assertions. An assertion like 'Transcript shows git log or source file reads were performed' would catch this gap.

eval_feedback.overall: All 6 assertions pass, but the eval has a structural weakness: there is no assertion requiring the executor to actually analyze a real codebase. The sandbox contained no source code, so the executor fabricated a progress-tracker.md and then 'synced' its own output. The behavioral patterns are correct, but the assertions cannot distinguish genuine syncdocs execution from a convincing simulation. Adding an assertion about performing actual codebase analysis (git log, source reads) would make the eval more discriminating.
